title: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

reviews.summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.
reviews.questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.

Questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.

Limitations:
The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning"

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a novel extension of PAC prediction sets to the few-shot meta-learning setting. It offers theoretically valid confidence sets with guarantees that hold across tasks, addressing limitations of prior work like Meta-CP. The approach is empirically validated on several datasets, demonstrating desirable properties such as reusable calibration data and satisfying the meta-PAC criterion where baselines fail. While the core idea combines existing techniques (PAC calibration and Meta-CP), the adaptation to the meta-learning context is a meaningful contribution.

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Well-Presented Core Idea:** The paper is generally well-written, and the theoretical setting for achieving PAC guarantees on average across new tasks is both appealing and practically useful.
2.  **Effective Calibration Strategy:** The proposed calibration strategy is theoretically sound and efficient. The technique of using two 1/2 factors (via Clopper-Pearson bounds) to avoid the overly conservative 1/N scaling factor found in some prior work is a clever and correct solution (implemented in PS-BINOM, Algorithm 2).
3.  **Strong Empirical Validation:** The experimental results are convincing. The validation effectively demonstrates that the proposed approach satisfies the meta-PAC coverage criterion across diverse tasks where baseline methods like standard Meta-CP fall short. The practical advantage of reusing a single calibration set across future tasks is clearly shown.

**Weaknesses and Concerns:**  
1.  **Readability and Notation:** The presentation, particularly in the technical sections, suffers from burdensome notation that hinders readability and comprehension. Simplifying the notation or providing more intuitive explanations would significantly improve accessibility.
2.  **Fixed Threshold Limitation:** A key limitation is the use of a single, fixed threshold for all downstream tasks. This approach lacks adaptivity to the inherent difficulty of individual tasks, a flexibility offered by Meta-CP's data-dependent quantiles. The paper would benefit from a deeper discussion of the trade-offs involved.
3.  **Calibration Data Requirement:** The method relies on the availability of hundreds of i.i.d. calibration tasks (N=500 in experiments). This requirement may be unrealistic or impractical in certain real-world meta-learning scenarios where such abundant task data is scarce.
4.  **Level of Novelty:** While well-executed and valuable, the technical novelty represents a relatively straightforward combination and adaptation of existing techniques (specifically, applying PAC calibration principles within the Meta-CP framework). The paper clearly advances the state-of-the-art but is an incremental step rather than a paradigm shift.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1.  **Improve Section 4 Clarity:** To address readability concerns, please add a high-level, step-by-step overview of the goals and core methodology at the beginning of Section 4. This roadmap would greatly aid the reader before delving into the dense technical details and notation.
2.  **Expand Conclusion with Limitations:** The conclusion should be expanded to more explicitly emphasize the method's limitations and underlying assumptions. Crucially, highlight that this approach is not a universal solution and relies on strong assumptions, notably the availability of a large pool of i.i.d. calibration tasks and the fixed threshold strategy. This contextualization is essential for readers and practitioners.

**Overall Recommendation:**  
This paper makes a solid contribution by effectively bridging PAC guarantees and meta-learning for prediction sets. The theoretical framework is sound, the calibration strategy is efficient, and the empirical results robustly demonstrate the method's advantages over relevant baselines in satisfying the meta-PAC criterion and enabling calibration set reuse. While the technical novelty is somewhat incremental and the presentation could be clearer, the work is technically solid and addresses an important problem with a practical solution. The limitations (fixed threshold, data requirements) are acknowledged but do not outweigh the paper's contributions. Therefore, I recommend **Weak Accept (Rating: 6)**. The suggested revisions, particularly regarding clarity in Section 4 and a more nuanced discussion of limitations in the conclusion, will strengthen the paper further.

Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning  

**Summary of Contribution:**  
This paper introduces a novel algorithm for constructing Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) prediction sets within a meta-learning framework. The method aims to adapt predictors to new tasks using minimal examples while providing formal statistical guarantees. Experimental validation across four diverse datasets demonstrates that the approach successfully satisfies PAC guarantees and achieves smaller prediction set sizes compared to baseline methods.  

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Clarity and Presentation:** The paper is well-structured and clearly articulated, making the technical contributions accessible.  
2.  **Solid Theoretical Foundation:** The proposed method is theoretically grounded, with a reasonable and sound analysis supporting the PAC guarantees.  
3.  **Experimental Breadth:** The evaluation covers a good range of domains and datasets, providing convincing evidence of the method's general applicability and effectiveness in reducing prediction set size under the PAC constraint.  

**Weaknesses and Concerns:**  
1.  **Suboptimal Parameter Reuse Strategy:** A significant limitation is the reuse of the meta-learned prediction set parameter (e.g., `epsilon_meta`) *unchanged* during task-specific adaptation. This static approach may not be optimal; intuitively, these parameters themselves should be adapted based on the specific support set of the new task to achieve tighter prediction sets.  
2.  **Limited Scope to Metric-Based Meta-Learning:** The validation is currently confined to metric-based approaches (e.g., prototypical networks). The paper lacks any implementation or evaluation demonstrating the method's efficacy or applicability with optimization-based meta-learning paradigms, such as MAML. This restricts the perceived generality of the contribution.  
3.  **Insufficient Task Complexity in Evaluation:** While experiments include mini-ImageNet, they are limited to 5-way classification. The method's robustness and scalability remain unverified for more challenging and realistic few-shot scenarios, such as 10-way or 20-way classification on the same dataset.  

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1.  **Refine the Adaptation Mechanism:** Investigate and incorporate mechanisms to adapt the meta-learned prediction set parameters (not just the predictor) during the task-specific adaptation phase. This could lead to more efficient and tighter prediction sets.  
2.  **Extend to Optimization-Based Meta-Learning:** To significantly broaden the impact and applicability of the work, the authors should implement and evaluate the proposed PAC prediction set method within optimization-based meta-learning frameworks (e.g., MAML) in future work.  
3.  **Evaluate on Harder Tasks:** Conduct additional experiments using mini-ImageNet (or similar) with higher-way classification (e.g., 10-way, 20-way) to rigorously assess the method's performance under increased task complexity and provide a more comprehensive evaluation.  

**Overall Assessment:**  
This work presents a valuable and technically sound contribution to the field of reliable meta-learning by introducing a PAC prediction set framework with theoretical guarantees. The core idea is promising, the analysis is rigorous, and the experimental results across multiple datasets are compelling, demonstrating a clear advantage over baselines. However, the identified limitations—particularly the static parameter reuse strategy, the confinement to metric-based meta-learning, and the lack of validation on harder tasks—prevent a stronger endorsement. These limitations point to important avenues for future research but do not fundamentally undermine the paper's core contribution and results.  

**Decision: Weak Accept (6)**  
The paper presents a solid, well-executed study with moderate-to-high impact potential. It addresses a relevant problem, provides theoretical grounding, and offers convincing empirical validation within its current scope. The weaknesses noted are significant but represent opportunities for extension rather than fatal flaws. I recommend acceptance, contingent on the authors addressing the weaknesses through future research as outlined above.

reviews.summary: This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.
reviews.questions: Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?
reviews.limitations: I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: Meta-PAC for Conformal Prediction in Meta-Learning  

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a novel meta-Probably Approximately Correct (mPAC) framework and associated algorithms to provide statistical guarantees for prediction sets in meta-learning across multiple task distributions. The work extends traditional PAC guarantees to handle heterogeneous distributions encountered in meta-learning, leveraging adaptation datasets without requiring importance weight estimation. The theoretical foundation is sound, the presentation is clear, and the approach offers valuable insights for conformal prediction in complex, multi-distribution settings.  

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Originality:** The proposal of the mPAC notion is a significant conceptual advancement. It effectively generalizes PAC guarantees to meta-learning scenarios involving multiple underlying distributions, addressing a gap in the current literature.  
2.  **Technical Quality & Clarity:** The theoretical analysis supporting the mPAC framework (particularly Theorem 1) is rigorous and clearly presented. Algorithm 1, employing a two-step strategy (computing per-distribution thresholds followed by a meta-threshold), is well-motivated and logically derived from the theory. The distinction from related work (e.g., covariate shift handling like [6]) due to the use of adaptation datasets is well-articulated.  
3.  **Significance:** This work provides important theoretical and practical tools for applying conformal prediction reliably in meta-learning. The ability to handle augmented calibration sets from multiple distributions and provide formal guarantees across these distributions is a valuable contribution to the field of uncertainty quantification in adaptive systems.  

**Weaknesses:**  
No major methodological or theoretical weaknesses were identified that undermine the core contributions.  

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1.  **Computational Cost Discussion:** While Algorithm 2 (involving grid search for the τ parameter) is presented, the computational implications of this grid search deserve more explicit discussion. Please elaborate on the practical cost and scalability of this step.  
2.  **Acceleration Strategies:** Building on the above, it would strengthen the paper to briefly discuss potential strategies or future research directions for accelerating the grid search in Algorithm 2, making the method more efficient for larger-scale applications.  

**Overall Assessment and Recommendation:**  
This paper presents a technically solid and original contribution by formalizing the mPAC guarantee and providing a theoretically grounded algorithm (Algorithm 1) to achieve it. The work addresses a relevant challenge in meta-learning for conformal prediction and offers valuable insights. The presentation is clear and the theoretical analysis is convincing. While a minor point regarding the computational aspects of Algorithm 2 could be expanded upon, this does not detract from the paper's core strengths and contributions.  

**Decision: 6: Weak Accept**  
I recommend acceptance. The paper introduces a novel and significant concept (mPAC) with sound theoretical backing and clear practical relevance. The minor suggestions for improvement regarding computational discussion do not outweigh the paper's technical merits and contribution to the field.

reviews.summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.
reviews.questions: What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?
reviews.limitations: I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

Summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a technically sound contribution to meta-learning by introducing the first two-stage training-conditional guarantee for prediction sets. While the technical approach is correct and experiments are comprehensive, significant concerns remain regarding the complexity of the proposed guarantee and improper attribution of prior work. After revisions addressing readability and attribution, the paper merits a weak accept.  

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Technical Soundness:** The core technical results and proofs are clearly correct. The application of binomial concentration twice (chaining the concentration argument) to achieve a training-conditional guarantee is methodologically solid.  
2.  **Comprehensive Evaluation:** The experimental validation is thorough and convincing, spanning multiple important domains including vision, language, and medical applications. This demonstrates the practical applicability of the method.  

**Weaknesses and Major Concerns:**  
1.  **Excessive Complexity and Poor Readability:** The central theoretical guarantee, involving a triply-nested probability structure, is presented in an overly complex manner. This creates an unnecessarily high cognitive load for readers and risks limiting the practical adoption of the method. The writing is often dense and difficult to follow.  
2.  **Critical Attribution Issues:** There is a major inaccuracy in attributing the concept of training-conditional prediction sets/PAC prediction sets solely to [3]. **Vovk [21] must be recognized as the originator of this concept.** Specifically, Proposition 2b in Vovk [21] is essentially equivalent to the method termed PS-BINOM in this work. Failing to properly credit Vovk [21] is a significant oversight.  
3.  **Limited Novelty:** While representing the second application of training-conditional conformal prediction to meta-learning, the technical contribution itself appears incremental when compared to the existing foundations laid by Vovk [21] and other meta conformal prediction works. The core innovation lies in the chaining argument, not the fundamental building blocks.  
4.  **Figure Clarity:** Figure 2 is particularly problematic. It contains extraneous information, lacks clear explanations, and fails to effectively communicate the intended message due to its clutter and poor organization.  

**Suggestions for Revision:**  
1.  **Correct Attribution:**  
    *   **Remove all statements** attributing the invention or sole development of training-conditional prediction sets/PAC prediction sets to [3].  
    *   **Explicitly credit Vovk [21]** as the originator of the training-conditional validity framework throughout the paper, particularly in the introduction and background sections.  
    *   **Clearly state** in the methodology section that PS-BINOM is effectively equivalent to Vovk's Proposition 2b in [21].  
2.  **Improve Readability and Clarity:**  
    *   **Significantly simplify the presentation** of the theoretical guarantee. Strive for intuitive explanations and minimize nested complexity in the main text; detailed derivations can be relegated to appendices.  
    *   **Thoroughly revise Figure 2:** Remove unnecessary elements, add clear labels and captions explaining each component and its role in the overall process, and ensure the figure effectively supports the textual description of the method.  
    *   Revise the writing throughout to enhance clarity and accessibility, especially in technical sections.  

**Overall Recommendation:**  
The paper introduces a novel two-stage training-conditional guarantee for meta-learning via a clever chaining of binomial concentration arguments and provides solid experimental validation across domains. However, the current presentation is hindered by excessive complexity and, more critically, major attribution errors concerning the foundational work of Vovk [21]. Addressing the attribution issues and significantly improving the readability (especially concerning the guarantee and Figure 2) are essential. **Once these revisions are made, the paper represents a sufficient technical contribution for publication, warranting a Weak Accept (6).** The technical soundness and empirical breadth support this decision, but the required revisions are substantial.

reviews.summary: The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.
reviews.questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.
reviews.limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.

Questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.

Limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: Revised Manuscript  

**Overall Summary:**  
This paper introduces a method for estimating prediction sets with PAC guarantees in meta-testing scenarios, employing adaptation and calibration phases. The approach is evaluated across four datasets (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel, CDC Heart Dataset) using prototypical networks as the score function. While the technical contribution—a theoretically grounded PAC-bound guarantee—is a strength, the presentation suffers from significant clarity issues. The manuscript is not self-contained, relies heavily on familiarity with prior work (Park et al., 2019), and lacks a coherent big-picture overview. These weaknesses substantially hinder accessibility and impact.  

**Strengths:**  
1. **Theoretical Guarantee:** The core method provides a rigorous PAC-bound guarantee for prediction sets, which is a valuable contribution to safe meta-learning.  

**Weaknesses and Major Concerns:**  
1. **Poor Presentation and Notation:**  
   - The notation is consistently confusing and at times misleading (e.g., undefined score functions, ambiguous prediction set definitions).  
   - Critical inconsistencies exist, such as differing inputs for score functions (Lines 106 vs. 108), which require explicit explanation.  
   - Figure 1 omits essential context (e.g., the pre-trained meta-model), diminishing its utility.  
2. **Lack of Big-Picture Clarity:** The manuscript fails to provide a clear, high-level narrative. Readers unfamiliar with Park et al. (2019) will struggle to contextualize the work.  
3. **Limited Empirical Evaluation:**  
   - Only one meta-model (prototypical networks) is tested, raising questions about generalizability.  
   - Results show Meta-PS achieves low prediction set error but occasionally larger set sizes versus Meta-CP. The trade-offs here lack thorough analysis.  
4. **Unjustified Design Choices:** The exclusive use of the PS-Binom estimator for τ is neither motivated nor compared to alternatives, leaving its impact unexplored.  

**Technical Feedback:**  
- The reliance on prototypical networks as the sole score function limits scope. Expanding evaluations to other architectures would strengthen claims.  
- The choice of datasets is appropriate, but results should explicitly discuss the implications of dataset diversity on method robustness.  
- Clarify the distinction between Meta-PS and Meta-CP in both formalism and empirical outcomes (e.g., when/why set sizes differ).  

**Decision:**  
**5: Borderline Accept**  
The paper’s PAC guarantee is a technically sound and valuable contribution. However, severe presentational flaws—including unclear notation, insufficient context, and narrow evaluation—significantly undermine its accessibility and reproducibility. While these weaknesses nearly justify rejection, the theoretical rigor and validation across multiple datasets tip the balance toward *borderline acceptance*. **Mandatory revisions** to address clarity issues are essential prior to publication.

title: Grounded Reinforcement Learning: Learning to Win the Game under Human Commands

reviews.summary:
Paper introduces a new task called “Grounded Reinforcement Learning” in which agents must be reward-maximizing with respect to some high-level language “constraint” (given by another agent/human). The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Results are not significantly better than baselines.
reviews.questions:
1) It is unclear how this approach is different from having standard RL with a combined state space over both command and game state.  As the command representation is not learnt - it seems unclear how this actually is different to a standard RL problem.

2) Can more justification be given to how difficult the opponent you play with is? Can you play with a harder agent to more clearly demonstrate the advantages of your method?

3) It is really unclear how the command token is meaningfully different to having an additional dimension within the game state. Can some justification be given to how either this representation is learnt or has any assemblance of language (is it compositional, have semantic or pragmatic properties).
reviews.limitations:
Addressing the above questions would be useful. 

I also believe actually using a language model would take great steps in demonstrating the effectiveness of your approach.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review: Grounded Reinforcement Learning with High-Level Language Commands  

### Summary  
This paper introduces Grounded Reinforcement Learning (GRL), a novel task requiring agents to maximize rewards while adhering to high-level language commands. The proposed RED method alternates between reinforcement learning (RL) and behavioral cloning (BC) updates, demonstrating improved performance in MiniRTS environments. While the problem statement is impactful and the human evaluation compelling, concerns regarding language representation and algorithmic novelty warrant attention.  

### Strengths  
1. **Novel Problem Formulation**: The introduction of GRL addresses a critical gap in safe and interpretable agent behavior, with significant implications for AI safety and human-AI collaboration.  
2. **Clarity and Presentation**: The paper is exceptionally well-written, with clear motivations and accessible explanations of the methodology.  
3. **Compelling Human Evaluation**: The human studies robustly validate the practical utility of the approach, strengthening the real-world relevance of GRL.  
4. **Safety Impact**: The work’s focus on aligning agent actions with human intent is timely and impactful for responsible AI development.  

### Weaknesses and Major Concerns  
1. **Oversimplified Language Representation**: Treating commands as one-hot encodings ignores linguistic pragmatics, syntax, and semantics. This severely limits the framework’s applicability to real-world language understanding (e.g., no compositional or contextual reasoning is modeled).  
2. **Limited Algorithmic Novelty**: RED effectively combines existing RL and BC techniques without introducing significant innovation in the fusion mechanism. The design lacks a clear theoretical or empirical distinction from simpler hybrid baselines.  
3. **Marginal Performance Gains**: Quantitative improvements over baselines (e.g., RL-only or BC-only) are not statistically significant, raising questions about RED’s practical advantage.  

### Suggestions for Improvement  
1. **Justify Opponent Difficulty**: Provide evidence that opponent strength in evaluations is sufficiently challenging to meaningfully differentiate RED from baselines.  
2. **Clarify Command Semantics**: Explicitly discuss how language commands differ from—and add value beyond—merely expanding the state space dimensions.  
3. **Integrate Language Modeling**: Replace one-hot encodings with learned embeddings (e.g., via transformers) to capture linguistic structure. This is essential for scalability and real-world relevance.  

### Overall Assessment  
This work makes a valuable contribution by formalizing the GRL task, which has substantial safety implications. The human evaluations and clear exposition are significant strengths. However, the simplistic treatment of language and incremental algorithmic design constrain the current impact. **I recommend acceptance (Rating: 7)** based on the importance of the problem statement and empirical validation, but urge the authors to address the limitations—particularly regarding language representation—in future work. The revisions suggested above would strengthen the paper’s technical rigor without requiring new experiments.  

**Decision: Accept**

reviews.summary:
This work focuses on grounded reinforcement learning. Generally, it means finding a conditional RL policy that can perfectly response to high-level command. 

In this paper, the authors proposed a constraned RL algorithm RED to (1) train an agent that is capable of winning strategy games and (2) follow most human instructions. Concretely, RED uses RL to learn explorative policy while uses BC to imitate human demonstration.

Experiment results suggest RED achieves command-following policies and higher winning rate.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths

* The idea is concise. Use BC to learn human behavior while use RL to explore better solution.
* Visualization and videos provided are mostly clear and contain necessary information.
* Fig1 is illustrative and helpful to explain what is grounded reinforcement learning. (It would be better if Fig 1 has no background color and in PDF format)
* Code is released.

### Weaknesses

* This work assumes reward from interactive environment. (limitation)
* The code has no detailed guidance and documentation.
* Though I like Figure 2 and other experiments, the main experiment results in Table 1 is confusing and non-convincing.
* The idea is not novel. Conditional imitation learning is a long lasting topics. Refer to "End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning"
* Many details are missing or confusing, such as Table 1 and Lagrangian method.
reviews.questions:
### Questions on method


Sec 4.3 says this work uses Lagrangian multiplier to solve the constrained optimization problem in Eq2. It turns out that, in my understanding, this is simply a weighted loss. A formal Lagrangian method should contain update process of the $\beta$, which is just ignored by this paper (or I miss, please point out). The later "iterative solution" also breaks the so called "Lagrangian method". 

---

Line 240 and Appendix A.2: Could you discuss more details on how you deal with action space? What does the "0/1 action for each unit" means? Who does the selection?

---

How to insert a NA command? IIUC, there exist a fixed set of possible commands right? So NA command should be just one more command in that set. You create embedding for each command in the set and than pass the embedding to the neural network.

---





### Questions on experiments

I can't understand table 1. IIUC, these four baselines have the policies that do not take command as input. Why they have different performance in Table 1 when applying different test commands? Please describe how you turn them as conditional policies. 

For example, since RL is trained without any objective to conduct distinguishable behaviors under different commands, why inserting different test commands will lead to different behavior?

---

I don't think Table 1 is convincing, especially when taking STD into account. 

Line 288: "Both RED and Joint policies substantially outperform Switch (BC) policy with sub-optimal commands (Random and Human Proxy), suggesting the conditioned behaviors are improved." This is a overclaimed statement. I can't draw this conclusion from Table 1.

---

I like Fig 2, which shows the dependency between commands and behaviors. Many "conditional RL" methods fail to prove that the conditional policy has casuality to the input command. This figure also shows that RED has much better "casuality" than RL.

My problem is that, in Figure 2, RL in 0% random commands achieves ~57% win rate. Does this value align with the RL performance with NA test command in Table 1?

Again, if RL is trained compelety without human command, then (1) where you can insert the command to the policy? and (2) why RL performance changes with the percentage of random commands?

---


Figure 6 says RL policy achieves similar win rate compared to RED. IIUC, RL policies are not conditioned on any human command. This result suggest that a human subject cooperates with RED trained conditional policy will lead to RL-comparable performance? This seems to be a violation to Table 1 "Oracle" result, which might lead to 

---

There is only one real gameplay footage provided (less than 2 minutes), with only 6 commands given, which is far less than convincing. Meanwhile, the commands seemed to be programmed/copy-pasted (I did not see any input lag), is there any real human intervention involved in recoding this video?

---

This game seems to be very complicated. Does your first intention of the application of this algorithm to be this high-level game? I understand the miniRTS environment is the only one that fitted the algorithm's need, but if you have alternatives (even if the performance is weak), would you also like to present them in the paper? Having diversity is a huge plus to your paper.








### Questions on paper




### Other questions or concerns



The [google drive link](https://drive.google.com/file/d/129ZV9CU6zUnp5B4v0p91Xaae8-M5zxHy/view) provided is **not anonymous**. This is a violation of the NeurIPS Code of Conduct (double-blind reviewing policy). Please action ASAP.

---

This paper has experiments involving 30 college students experiments, are attendees aware of their rights and responsibilities? A more explicit Ethics Statement should be included.

---

Conditional BC is not a new idea. Please refer to this work: End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning

---

Appendix D, as said in the checklist, has no information on human experiment. You moved them to Appendix E.

---

The website given in the appendix is very informative and I like the real-time gameplay footage, but there are several problems I want to point out:
- Code link is broken.
- Many grammar errors, will not be present here (plural or singular, tense, etc)
reviews.limitations:
The authors provide limitation section.

GRL is an interesting field. How to improve human-AI cooperation in test-time, how to learn a better human proxy, and how to further encourage, illustrate, quantify the causity between command and behaviors might be future directions.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Grounded Reinforcement Learning with Command-Conditioned Policies"

**Overall Assessment:**  
This paper presents Grounded Reinforcement Learning (GRL) and the RED algorithm, aiming to train agents that win strategy games while adhering to human commands by combining reinforcement learning (RL) with behavior cloning (BC). The core idea is concise, and the visualizations effectively illustrate the GRL concept. However, significant concerns regarding novelty, the strength of the experimental evidence, and implementation clarity temper the contribution. While the technical approach is fundamentally sound and the work has potential impact, the issues identified necessitate major revisions. Therefore, I recommend a **Weak Accept (Rating 6)**. The paper presents a technically solid contribution with moderate-to-high potential impact, but no major flaws preclude eventual publication after addressing the concerns below.

**Major Strengths:**  
1.  **Concise and Promising Core Idea:** The fundamental concept of combining BC to capture human-like behavior with RL for strategic exploration and improvement (GRL framework) is presented clearly and holds intuitive appeal. The RED algorithm is a logical instantiation of this idea.
2.  **Effective Visual Communication:** Figure 1 provides an excellent and clear illustration of the GRL concepts. The inclusion of videos is also highly valuable for understanding the agents' behavior and command-following capabilities.
3.  **Code Availability:** The release of code is commendable and essential for reproducibility and future work in this area.

**Major Weaknesses and Concerns:**  
1.  **Novelty Concerns:** The paper's core methodology appears to bear significant resemblance to conditional imitation learning (CIL) frameworks. The distinction between GRL/RED and existing CIL approaches, particularly concerning how commands condition the policy, is inadequately articulated. The claims of novelty require substantial bolstering and clearer differentiation from prior art.
2.  **Insufficient Experimental Evidence:**  
    *   **Table 1 Results:** The primary results presented in Table 1 are unconvincing. The lack of standard deviation reporting, extremely high win rates for baselines under "NA" commands, and apparent misalignment between RL win rates in Fig 2 and Table 1 raise serious doubts about the statistical significance and robustness of the claimed improvements. It is also unclear how the baselines (PPO, BC, BC+RL) were adapted to be command-conditional for a fair comparison in Table 1; this crucial detail is missing.
    *   **Limited Gameplay Footage:** The provided gameplay footage demonstrating human command following is currently insufficient to robustly evaluate the core claim of effective command grounding. More varied and extensive footage is needed.
3.  **Implementation Clarity and Detail:**  
    *   **Lagrangian Method:** The description of the Lagrangian multiplier method for constraint satisfaction is confusing. The implementation presented (Eq. 4-5) appears functionally equivalent to a simple weighted sum of losses (BC + RL), contradicting the stated use of a Lagrangian. This must be clarified or corrected.
    *   **Command Implementation:** The architecture for processing commands (via LSTM embeddings) and integrating them into the policy to condition action selection needs more detailed explanation. Specifically, clarifying how "NA" (no command) is implemented within this architecture is essential.
    *   **Action Space:** The description of the action space (0/1 selection per unit) is unclear and requires elaboration.
4.  **Ethical Omission:** The paper involves human studies (collecting demonstrations). The lack of an explicit ethics statement regarding participant consent, data handling, and approval is a significant oversight that must be addressed.
5.  **Reproducibility Concerns:**  
    *   **Code Documentation:** The released code suffers from inadequate documentation and guidance, hindering reproducibility and adoption.
    *   **Anonymity Violation:** The inclusion of a Google Drive link in the supplementary materials potentially compromises the anonymity of the review process and should be removed immediately.

**Suggestions for Revision:**  
1.  **Substantially Strengthen Novelty Claim:** Provide a thorough discussion comparing and contrasting GRL/RED with conditional imitation learning and related command-conditional RL approaches. Clearly articulate the novel aspects.
2.  **Revise and Expand Experimental Section:**  
    *   **Table 1:** Report standard deviations for all results. Provide a detailed explanation of how *all* baselines in Table 1 were made command-conditional. Ensure results align across figures/tables (e.g., Fig 2 vs Table 1 RL). Perform a rigorous statistical analysis of the results.
    *   **Gameplay Footage:** Include significantly more gameplay footage demonstrating the agent successfully following a diverse range of human commands in various scenarios.
3.  **Clarify Methodological Details:**  
    *   Provide a precise and accurate description of the constraint handling method (Lagrangian vs. weighted loss). Correct the text or implementation as necessary.
    *   Explain the policy architecture in detail, specifically how commands (including "NA") are processed (LSTM) and used to condition action selection.
    *   Elaborate on the action space design.
4.  **Add Ethics Statement:** Include a clear ethics statement detailing participant consent procedures, data anonymization practices, and institutional review board approval (if applicable) for the human demonstration collection.
5.  **Improve Reproducibility:**  
    *   Significantly enhance the code documentation and provide clear usage instructions.
    *   **Remove the Google Drive link** from the supplementary materials to preserve anonymity.
6.  **Proofreading:** Carefully proofread the supplementary materials to correct grammatical errors.

**Conclusion:**  
The paper proposes an interesting integration of BC and RL for command-conditioned agents in strategy games, supported by clear visualizations and released code. However, concerns about novelty, the strength of the empirical evidence (especially Table 1), implementation clarity (Lagrangian, command conditioning), ethical disclosure, and reproducibility (code docs, anonymity breach) are substantial. Addressing these weaknesses through major revisions, as outlined above, is crucial for the paper to realize its potential impact. I recommend **Weak Accept (Rating 6)** contingent on the authors successfully addressing these revisions. The core technical idea is sound, and the work has merit, but the current presentation has significant shortcomings.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This work focuses on grounded reinforcement learning. Generally, it means finding a conditional RL policy that can perfectly response to high-level command. 

In this paper, the authors proposed a constraned RL algorithm RED to (1) train an agent that is capable of winning strategy games and (2) follow most human instructions. Concretely, RED uses RL to learn explorative policy while uses BC to imitate human demonstration.

Experiment results suggest RED achieves command-following policies and higher winning rate.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
### Strengths

* The idea is concise. Use BC to learn human behavior while use RL to explore better solution.
* Visualization and videos provided are mostly clear and contain necessary information.
* Fig1 is illustrative and helpful to explain what is grounded reinforcement learning. (It would be better if Fig 1 has no background color and in PDF format)
* Code is released.

### Weaknesses

* This work assumes reward from interactive environment. (limitation)
* The code has no detailed guidance and documentation.
* Though I like Figure 2 and other experiments, the main experiment results in Table 1 is confusing and non-convincing.
* The idea is not novel. Conditional imitation learning is a long lasting topics. Refer to "End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning"
* Many details are missing or confusing, such as Table 1 and Lagrangian method.

Questions:
### Questions on method


Sec 4.3 says this work uses Lagrangian multiplier to solve the constrained optimization problem in Eq2. It turns out that, in my understanding, this is simply a weighted loss. A formal Lagrangian method should contain update process of the $\beta$, which is just ignored by this paper (or I miss, please point out). The later "iterative solution" also breaks the so called "Lagrangian method". 

---

Line 240 and Appendix A.2: Could you discuss more details on how you deal with action space? What does the "0/1 action for each unit" means? Who does the selection?

---

How to insert a NA command? IIUC, there exist a fixed set of possible commands right? So NA command should be just one more command in that set. You create embedding for each command in the set and than pass the embedding to the neural network.

---





### Questions on experiments

I can't understand table 1. IIUC, these four baselines have the policies that do not take command as input. Why they have different performance in Table 1 when applying different test commands? Please describe how you turn them as conditional policies. 

For example, since RL is trained without any objective to conduct distinguishable behaviors under different commands, why inserting different test commands will lead to different behavior?

---

I don't think Table 1 is convincing, especially when taking STD into account. 

Line 288: "Both RED and Joint policies substantially outperform Switch (BC) policy with sub-optimal commands (Random and Human Proxy), suggesting the conditioned behaviors are improved." This is a overclaimed statement. I can't draw this conclusion from Table 1.

---

I like Fig 2, which shows the dependency between commands and behaviors. Many "conditional RL" methods fail to prove that the conditional policy has casuality to the input command. This figure also shows that RED has much better "casuality" than RL.

My problem is that, in Figure 2, RL in 0% random commands achieves ~57% win rate. Does this value align with the RL performance with NA test command in Table 1?

Again, if RL is trained compelety without human command, then (1) where you can insert the command to the policy? and (2) why RL performance changes with the percentage of random commands?

---


Figure 6 says RL policy achieves similar win rate compared to RED. IIUC, RL policies are not conditioned on any human command. This result suggest that a human subject cooperates with RED trained conditional policy will lead to RL-comparable performance? This seems to be a violation to Table 1 "Oracle" result, which might lead to 

---

There is only one real gameplay footage provided (less than 2 minutes), with only 6 commands given, which is far less than convincing. Meanwhile, the commands seemed to be programmed/copy-pasted (I did not see any input lag), is there any real human intervention involved in recoding this video?

---

This game seems to be very complicated. Does your first intention of the application of this algorithm to be this high-level game? I understand the miniRTS environment is the only one that fitted the algorithm's need, but if you have alternatives (even if the performance is weak), would you also like to present them in the paper? Having diversity is a huge plus to your paper.








### Questions on paper




### Other questions or concerns



The [google drive link](https://drive.google.com/file/d/129ZV9CU6zUnp5B4v0p91Xaae8-M5zxHy/view) provided is **not anonymous**. This is a violation of the NeurIPS Code of Conduct (double-blind reviewing policy). Please action ASAP.

---

This paper has experiments involving 30 college students experiments, are attendees aware of their rights and responsibilities? A more explicit Ethics Statement should be included.

---

Conditional BC is not a new idea. Please refer to this work: End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning

---

Appendix D, as said in the checklist, has no information on human experiment. You moved them to Appendix E.

---

The website given in the appendix is very informative and I like the real-time gameplay footage, but there are several problems I want to point out:
- Code link is broken.
- Many grammar errors, will not be present here (plural or singular, tense, etc)

Limitations:
The authors provide limitation section.

GRL is an interesting field. How to improve human-AI cooperation in test-time, how to learn a better human proxy, and how to further encourage, illustrate, quantify the causity between command and behaviors might be future directions.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

The review acknowledges the method's conciseness and visual clarity but raises concerns about novelty, experimental evidence, and implementation details.
